annotations.description: The problem is that Section 4.1 reports an incorrect number of images for a dataset, leading to data inconsistency that needs correction.

predictions.description: Strength: The paper identifies a concrete limitation in prior CoSOD methods—using heuristic raw inter-image associations only as guidance for feature optimization—and proposes a more explicit alternative by learning deep features directly from association tensors.

Strength: The method is modular and conceptually well structured: PAG improves association quality progressively, CAC reduces association volume, and OCC provides training supervision for correspondence estimation. This decomposition helps isolate methodological contributions.

Strength: The manuscript provides nontrivial ablations for PAG vs. SAG, CAC vs. SAC, and OCC vs. FCC, which supports the claimed utility of each component rather than presenting only a monolithic model.
Strength: Evaluation under multiple training-set combinations is a methodological plus because it addresses fairness when comparing to prior CoSOD methods trained on different data mixtures.
Strength: The paper compares against several recent strong baselines, including transformer-based methods, which strengthens the empirical positioning of the method.

Strength: The introduction of semantic correspondence as an auxiliary mechanism for CoSOD is methodologically interesting and potentially broadens the task formulation beyond standard consensus modeling.
Strength: The object-aware cycle consistency loss is a sensible refinement over full-image correspondence losses, because background pixels often violate the assumption of valid correspondence.

Weakness: The core hyperassociation tensor is extremely high dimensional (effectively 6D over group and spatial axes, plus layer dimension), yet the paper does not provide sufficient complexity analysis beyond one MACs number for aggregation networks. Memory use, runtime, and scaling with image size/group size are not adequately characterized.

Weakness: Efficiency claims are only partially substantiated. CAC reduces MACs from 91.38G to 77.19G, which is a modest reduction relative to the complexity of the full design, and no wall-clock speed, peak GPU memory, or inference-time comparisons are reported.

Weakness: The aggregation network over hyperassociations is difficult to assess and somewhat underspecified. The number of aggregation layers, channel sizes, downsampling factors, and exact tensor reshaping/indexing conventions are not fully detailed in the manuscript excerpt, limiting reproducibility.

Weakness: Several central design choices remain heuristic rather than principled or validated: ReLU on cosine similarities, summation across layers before argmax for initial correspondence, choice of K=9, square-neighborhood condensation, and averaging across the second image dimension in Eq. 9.

Weakness: The semantic correspondence component is described as a pretext task with unsupervised supervision, but OCC uses ground-truth co-saliency masks during training to restrict the loss to foreground. This is not fully unsupervised and should be framed more carefully.

Weakness: Using ground-truth masks in the correspondence loss may inflate training performance and weakens the practical independence of the auxiliary task. It also raises the question of whether gains come from better association modeling or simply stronger privileged supervision.

Weakness: The reliance on VGG-16 as backbone may limit the generality of conclusions. It is unclear whether the proposed association-learning strategy remains effective with stronger modern backbones, or whether improvements partly reflect a weak baseline encoder.

Weakness: The baseline for ablation is an FPN plus R2R module, which may not be the strongest or most natural baseline against current CoSOD systems. Stronger ablations against recent transformer-style aggregation backbones would better validate the claimed methodological advantage.

Weakness: The paper claims robustness in complex scenarios, but does not appear to include targeted stress tests or subset analyses for specific failure modes such as large pose variation, clutter, foreground-background similarity, or category diversity.

Weakness: The method processes groups of related images with fixed batch/group size N=6 in training, but there is little discussion of sensitivity to group size or generalization to variable-sized groups, which is important for CoSOD methodology.

Weakness: The correspondence estimation procedure begins from maximum-similarity initialization derived from the same association tensor that the method argues is unreliable in complex scenarios. This creates some conceptual tension and may make CAC sensitive to poor initial matches.

Weakness: The condensation operation may discard informative nonlocal or multimodal correspondences by restricting attention to a single predicted correspondence plus local neighborhood. This could hurt performance on articulated objects or repeated parts, and no analysis of such failure cases is given.

Weakness: The use of bilinear interpolation and 2D convolutions over selected tensor slices is plausible, but the paper does not justify why these operations are the right inductive bias for high-order association reasoning compared with alternatives such as attention or true higher-dimensional convolutions.

Weakness: The manuscript lacks statistical rigor in reporting results. No variance over runs, confidence intervals, or significance analysis are provided, making it hard to judge robustness of gains, especially for smaller improvements in some ablations.

Weakness: There are multiple presentation/reproducibility issues in the text: incomplete author line, placeholder tokens like [AUTHOR_1], [JOURNAL], duplicated or misplaced equations, and awkward equation ordering. These do not invalidate the method but reduce confidence in methodological precision.

Weakness: The OCC loss computes image-cycle consistency via SSIM on masked images, but the text does not discuss differentiability/stability of the warping with discrete correspondence coordinates, nor whether correspondence coordinates are continuous, rounded, or sampled.

Weakness: The manuscript does not make clear whether correspondence learning is only used during training or also incurs inference cost, which matters for assessing practicality and the true test-time burden of CAC.

Weakness: The claim of novelty that 'this has never been explored in previous CoSOD methods' is plausible for direct association-feature learning, but methodologically the design is heavily inspired by cost-volume aggregation literature from adjacent tasks. The true novelty is thus in task adaptation rather than entirely new modeling machinery.

annotations.description: The problem is that Section 4.1 reports an incorrect number of images for a dataset, leading to data inconsistency that needs correction.

predictions.description: The aggregation network equations are dimensionally inconsistent and do not clearly define a valid computation from the 7D hyperassociation tensor to the claimed association feature tensor. In Eq. (6)-(8), 2D convolutions are applied to slices of tensors with incompatible leading dimensions (e.g., N x H x W x C' treated as a 2D feature map), indices are reused inconsistently, and the text claims elimination of the target H x W dimension while later retaining N x C over target images before averaging.

The correspondence-induced condensation module is not rigorously specified as a differentiable or even well-defined operation. The method uses argmax to select an initial correspondence, predicts continuous offsets, then performs index selection a_s^j(n_s^j) without explaining coordinate discretization, interpolation, boundary handling, or gradient flow through the sampling step.

The object-aware cycle consistency loss uses ground-truth segmentation masks during training to restrict correspondence supervision to co-salient pixels. This injects explicit target annotations into the auxiliary task that is presented as unsupervised/self-supervised correspondence learning.

The paper claims computational burden reduction from CAC, but the proposed procedure appears to require first computing full raw associations to identify maxima and then constructing condensed associations. Since (h_j^0,w_j^0) is selected from a_s^j that itself comes from the full similarity tensor, the claimed reduction is not clearly supported unless full hyperassociations are never materialized. This distinction is not explained.

The manuscript alternates between describing hyperassociations as 6D and 7D tensors. For example, the related-work comparison says '6D hyperassociations,' while Eq. (4) defines A_s with an additional L_s layer dimension, making it effectively 7D. Similar confusion appears in later tensor descriptions.

There is an inconsistency in notation and feature dimensions for the aggregation output. Eq. (5) maps to N x H_s x W_s x C_s, but later the intermediate aggregated association is denoted F_s^{A'} in R^{N x H_s x W_s x N x C}, and then averaged over the second N dimension. The relation between C and C_s is unclear.

The training protocol is insufficiently specified for group-based data sampling. The batch size is stated as N=6, but N is also the number of related images in a group throughout the formulation. The manuscript does not explain how groups with variable cardinality are handled during training/evaluation, whether images are subsampled, padded, or grouped dynamically.

The OCC loss averages over all i,j including i=j in Eq. (16), but self-correspondence pairs are trivial and may distort the auxiliary objective. The paper does not state whether diagonal pairs are excluded in implementation.

The manuscript provides no statistical characterization of performance variability: no repeated runs, confidence intervals, or significance testing, despite claiming notable gains over prior work and using multiple training-set combinations.

Fairness of comparison is only partially established. The authors compare against official saliency maps from prior works trained under potentially different backbones, resolutions, post-processing, and data protocols, while training their own method under several dataset combinations to align with selected baselines. The exact alignment for each comparison is not fully documented in the manuscript.

annotations.description: The problem is that Section 4.1 reports an incorrect number of images for a dataset, leading to data inconsistency that needs correction.

predictions.description: Core tensor formulations in the aggregation network (Eqs. 5-8) are internally inconsistent. The hyperassociation tensor is defined as 7D, but subsequent 2D convolution operations have mismatched indexing and dimensional specifications. The claimed dimensional reductions do not follow rigorously from the stated operations. This makes the central architectural contribution impossible to verify or reproduce from the paper alone.

Training group formation protocol is unspecified. The paper does not explain how groups of N=6 related images are sampled from datasets with variable numbers of images per class, how variable-length groups are handled, or whether training and inference group sizes match. For a method that builds N×N inter-image associations, this is a core experimental design detail.

Hyperassociation tensor computational feasibility is not convincingly demonstrated. Memory footprint, runtime, and how the full dense tensor is materialized or streamed are not reported. The paper only reports MACs for the aggregation submodule, not the full model including correspondence estimation and OCC loss overhead.

The condensation operator (Eq. 15) does not specify how non-integer coordinates are handled (rounding vs. bilinear sampling), whether gradients pass through the selection, or boundary handling. This is central to the CAC and OCC modules.

The auxiliary correspondence loss (OCC) uses ground-truth segmentation masks to gate the cycle-consistency objective (Eq. 16), but the paper frames the correspondence module as learning from raw associations. This weakens the novelty claim and the fairness of comparisons to unsupervised correspondence methods.

Training data pipeline fairness: the paper uses a synthesis strategy for DUTS class and trains multiple variants without sufficiently detailing whether all compared methods used the same synthesized data, preprocessing, and group construction.
Hyperparameter K=9 in CAC controls the sparsity of the condensed hyperassociation tensor and directly affects both efficiency claims and association quality. No sensitivity analysis is provided.

OCC loss averages over all i,j pairs including self-pairs (i=j), where cycle consistency is trivial. No exclusion of diagonal terms is mentioned, potentially diluting or biasing the auxiliary training signal.
Numerous notation and equation-editing problems throughout the manuscript (duplicated passages, undefined symbols, inconsistent naming, garbled text) reduce reproducibility and clarity.
No variance estimates, repeated runs, or statistical significance tests are reported for the benchmark comparisons or ablation studies.
Heuristic initialization for correspondence uses argmax over summed layerwise associations without justifying summation across layers with potentially different scales/distributions.
